questions.prompt: An azimuth of 247 degrees 32 minutes corresponds to which bearing?
questions.rationale: 247 deg 32 min lies in the SW quadrant (180 to 270). Subtract 180 to get 67 deg 32 min; quadrant SW gives S 67 deg 32 min W.

questions.prompt: The back-azimuth of a line whose forward azimuth is 73 deg 15 min is closest to:
questions.rationale: Back-azimuth = forward + 180 deg (mod 360). 73 deg 15 min + 180 deg = 253 deg 15 min.

questions.prompt: A bearing of S 12 deg 40 min E has which corresponding azimuth from north?
questions.rationale: SE quadrant: azimuth = 180 - bearing angle = 180 deg - 12 deg 40 min = 167 deg 20 min.

questions.prompt: Why is stating 'bearing N 45 deg 00 min E' insufficient on a survey plat without further qualification?

choices.text: Bearings must always be given in azimuth form on plats.
The basis of bearings (grid, geodetic, magnetic, assumed) is required so the line can be retraced.
Indiana law forbids stating a bearing to less than one second.
Bearings are only valid for distances under 500 ft.

questions.rationale: A bearing means nothing without a stated meridian basis. 865 IAC 1-12-11 requires the plat to state the basis of bearings.

questions.prompt: A line has a geodetic azimuth of 178 deg 10 min and a grid convergence of +0 deg 45 min (grid north east of true north) at the instrument. What is the grid azimuth of the line?
questions.rationale: When grid north is east of true (geodetic) north (positive convergence), grid azimuth = geodetic azimuth - convergence. 178 deg 10 min - 0 deg 45 min = 177 deg 25 min.

questions.prompt: A total station reports a slope distance of 200.00 ft and a zenith angle of 87 deg 00 min 00 sec. The horizontal distance is closest to:
A measured ground distance of 1000.000 ft is to be converted to grid for State Plane reporting. The combined scale factor at the project is 0.99988. The grid distance is:
Which of the following is a SYSTEMATIC error in EDM measurement?

choices.text: Mis-pointing of the instrument crosshair on a single shot.
Random scintillation of the air path between instrument and prism.
An incorrect prism constant entered into the data collector.
Transposing two digits while writing the distance in the field book.

questions.rationale: An incorrect prism constant biases every distance by the same amount, the defining trait of a systematic error. The others are random or blunder.

questions.prompt: Atmospheric ppm corrections on an EDM primarily account for variations in:

choices.text: Magnetic declination at the project location.
Speed of light through air, which depends on temperature, pressure, and humidity.
Refraction of GNSS signals through the ionosphere.

questions.rationale: EDM distances rely on the speed of light through air. Temperature and pressure (and to a smaller extent humidity) change air refractive index, so ppm corrections are applied.

questions.prompt: A slope distance of 1532.45 ft is measured at a vertical angle of +3 deg 25 min from horizontal. The vertical difference between instrument and target axes is closest to:
questions.rationale: dV = S * sin(V) where V is the vertical angle from horizontal. 1532.45 * sin(3 deg 25 min) = 1532.45 * 0.05966 = 91.45 ft.

questions.prompt: Which projection is used by the Indiana State Plane coordinate system?
questions.rationale: Indiana State Plane (both East FIPS 1301 and West FIPS 1302) uses Lambert Conformal Conic on NAD83.

questions.prompt: The current National Spatial Reference System vertical datum for the contiguous United States is:
questions.rationale: NAVD88 is the current US orthometric datum. NGVD29 is its predecessor.

questions.prompt: A GNSS receiver reports an ellipsoid height of 875.20 ft at a point where the Geoid18 model gives a geoid height of -114.30 ft (geoid below ellipsoid). The NAVD88 orthometric height is:
questions.rationale: H_orthometric = h_ellipsoid - N_geoid = 875.20 - (-114.30) = 989.50 ft.

questions.prompt: Hamilton County, Indiana, lies in which State Plane zone?
questions.rationale: Hamilton County (Carmel, Fishers, Noblesville, Westfield) is in the eastern half of Indiana, in the Indiana East zone (FIPS 1301).

questions.prompt: If a project's mean elevation factor is 0.99996 and the grid scale factor at the project latitude is 0.99993, the combined scale factor is closest to:
questions.rationale: CSF = grid scale * elevation factor = 0.99993 * 0.99996 = 0.999890 (to six places).

questions.prompt: For a traverse leg of bearing N 30 deg 00 min E and distance 500.00 ft, the latitude (N-S component) is closest to:
questions.rationale: Latitude = D * cos(bearing) = 500 * cos(30 deg) = 500 * 0.8660 = 433.01 ft. NE quadrant gives positive latitude.

questions.prompt: For the same leg (N 30 deg 00 min E, 500.00 ft), the departure (E-W component) is closest to:
questions.rationale: Departure = D * sin(bearing) = 500 * sin(30 deg) = 250.00 ft. NE quadrant gives positive departure.

questions.prompt: A four-cornered parcel has coordinates (in ft, N then E): (0, 0), (300, 0), (300, 200), (0, 200). The area is:
questions.rationale: Rectangle 300 ft by 200 ft = 60,000 sq ft. Verifiable with the shoelace formula.

questions.prompt: A closed traverse has total perimeter 2400 ft and linear misclosure 0.20 ft. The linear precision is approximately:
questions.rationale: Precision = perimeter / misclosure = 2400 / 0.20 = 12,000, expressed as 1:12,000.

questions.prompt: Using the compass (Bowditch) rule, a traverse leg of length 600 ft on a traverse with perimeter 3000 ft and a latitude misclosure of +0.15 ft receives what latitude correction?
questions.rationale: Correction = -(closure) * (L_leg / perimeter) = -(+0.15) * (600 / 3000) = -0.030 ft. Sign is opposite of closure to drive net to zero.

questions.prompt: A circular curve has radius R = 500 ft and central angle delta = 30 deg. The tangent length T is closest to:
For the same curve (R = 500 ft, delta = 30 deg), the arc length L is closest to:
For a circular curve with R = 1000 ft and delta = 12 deg 00 min, the chord length C is closest to:
For a curve with R = 1000 ft and delta = 12 deg, the middle ordinate M is closest to:

Two curves of opposite hand share a common tangent (reverse curve). Curve 1: R = 600 ft, delta = 25 deg. Curve 2: R = 800 ft, delta = 25 deg. The total tangent distance from PI1 back to PI2 along the common-tangent direction is closest to:
questions.rationale: Each curve's tangent T_i = R_i * tan(delta_i/2). T1 = 600 * tan(12.5 deg) = 133.06 ft. T2 = 800 * tan(12.5 deg) = 177.34 ft. Sum = 310.40 ft.

questions.prompt: In the classical hierarchy of evidence for boundary determination, which is generally given the LEAST weight?
questions.rationale: The dignity-of-calls hierarchy favors monuments first, then adjoiners, course, distance, with area and coordinates last.

questions.prompt: A boundary surveyor's primary task on a retracement survey is to:

choices.text: Create new boundary lines that resolve any apparent overlap.
Locate the lines created by the original surveyor, following the original footsteps.
Establish coordinates on the current State Plane realization regardless of monuments.
File a complaint with the county court whenever evidence conflicts.

questions.rationale: Retracement reconstructs an existing boundary; it does not create new ones. The original surveyor's lines, marked by monuments, are the law of that survey.

questions.prompt: Grantor conveys Tract A in 1990 with a metes-and-bounds description. In 2000, the same grantor conveys adjoining Tract B; when plotted, the two descriptions overlap by 6 ft. Assuming no other controlling evidence, who keeps the disputed 6 ft strip?

choices.text: Tract B owner, because the 2000 deed is more recent and reflects updated measurements.
Tract A owner, because the 1990 deed is senior.
The disputed area is divided equally between the two owners.
The disputed area reverts to the original grantor.

questions.rationale: Senior rights doctrine: the earlier-recorded deed gets its full description; the later (junior) deed takes what is left.

questions.prompt: On a recorded subdivision plat, lots 1 through 20 are created at the same instant. When a record gap between lots 8 and 9 is discovered, the gap is generally:

choices.text: Assigned to whichever lot was sold first.
Pro-rated between the affected lots, since they are simultaneously created.
Awarded to the developer who recorded the plat.
Returned to the township or county.

questions.rationale: Lots within a recorded plat are simultaneous; senior/junior does not apply among them. Gaps and overlaps are resolved by proration.

questions.prompt: A surveyor finds an iron pin 1.3 ft from the platted dimension at a recorded subdivision corner. Field evidence (occupation, neighbor recognition) suggests the pin was set at the time of the original plat. The surveyor's correct conclusion is:

choices.text: Reject the pin and set a new monument at the platted dimension.
Hold the original pin as controlling; the original monument controls the platted dimension.
Average the position of the pin and the platted dimension.
Refuse to issue a plat until the discrepancy is litigated.

questions.rationale: Original undisturbed monuments control over the recorded measurements. The platted dimension is evidence of intent but yields to a found original monument.

questions.prompt: Which is the correct distinction between riparian and littoral rights?

choices.text: Riparian applies to flowing watercourses; littoral applies to non-flowing water bodies such as lakes.
Riparian applies to lakes; littoral applies to rivers.
Both terms mean the same thing.
Riparian applies to navigable waters only; littoral to non-navigable only.

questions.rationale: Riparian = flowing (river, stream). Littoral = static (lake, ocean).

questions.prompt: In Indiana, the boundary of a parcel adjoining a non-navigable stream typically extends to:

choices.text: The edge of the water at the time of survey.
The far bank of the stream.

questions.rationale: On non-navigable Indiana streams, the bed belongs to the adjoining landowners to the thread of the stream. Navigable waters have state-owned beds.

questions.prompt: A river slowly deposits soil along the bank of a parcel over decades, expanding the dry land by 10 ft. The new soil belongs to:

choices.text: The riparian owner of the abutting parcel, by accretion.
Whoever first uses the new ground for ten years.

questions.rationale: Accretion: gradual deposit of soil belongs to the abutting riparian owner. Avulsion (sudden change) does not move the boundary.

questions.prompt: Indiana adverse possession (IC 32-21-7) requires that the claimant's possession be all of the following, EXCEPT:

choices.text: With permission of the record owner

questions.rationale: Adverse possession requires possession that is open, notorious, hostile (without permission), continuous, and exclusive for the statutory period (10 years in Indiana).

questions.prompt: An interior section corner of the PLSS for which no physical evidence and no collateral testimony can be found is classified as:
questions.rationale: BLM Manual: existent = found undisturbed; obliterated = position recoverable from collateral evidence; lost = no direct or indirect evidence remains.

questions.prompt: Per the BLM Manual, a LOST interior section corner is reconstructed using:

choices.text: Bearing-bearing intersection from adjacent quarter corners
Whatever method the local county surveyor prefers

questions.rationale: Interior section corners (where four sections meet) are reconstructed by double proportionate measurement using the four nearest controlling corners.

questions.prompt: A lost standard quarter corner along a single section line is reconstructed by:

choices.text: Single proportionate measurement between the two controlling section corners on that line.
Setting at the platted record distance from only one controlling corner.
Adopting any iron pin found within 5 ft of the record position.

questions.rationale: Quarter corners along a line are line corners; they are restored by single proportionate measurement.

questions.prompt: Indiana law (IC 36-2-12) places statutory responsibility for perpetuating PLSS section corners on the:
questions.rationale: IC 36-2-12 assigns the county surveyor the duty to perpetuate corners and maintain the section corner tie card index.

questions.prompt: A surveyor finds a stone monument at an Indiana section corner with weathered chisel marks consistent with the original GLO survey. There is no cap or modern identification. The original GLO field notes describe a stone at this location. The correct classification is:

choices.text: Lost, must be reconstructed by double proportion.
Cannot be used; only iron pins with caps are valid in Indiana.

questions.rationale: An undisturbed original GLO stone monument, identifiable by chisel marks matching the original notes, is an existent original corner and controls.

questions.prompt: Per 865 IAC 1-12-10, the minimum acceptable iron monument for a boundary corner in Indiana is:
questions.rationale: 865 IAC 1-12-10 specifies a minimum 1/2 in. diameter and 24 in. length for iron monuments. Other materials are permitted for specific applications.

questions.prompt: 865 IAC 1-12-13 requires an Indiana licensed surveyor to retain survey records for:

choices.text: Three years from the date of the survey.
Seven years from the date of the survey.
The life of the license plus five years.

questions.rationale: 865 IAC 1-12-13: records must be retained for the life of the license plus 5 years.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is NOT required by 865 IAC 1-12-11 to appear on a survey plat?

choices.text: Names of adjoining owners or subdivisions
Surveyor's seal, signature, and license number

questions.rationale: 865 IAC 1-12-11 lists required plat content. Seal/signature/license number, basis of bearings, and adjoiner names are required. The surveyor's home address is not.

questions.prompt: Per 865 IAC 1-12, a monument cap on a set boundary corner must include:
questions.rationale: 865 IAC 1-12-10 requires identification of the surveyor by license number (or firm name with license number) on the cap.

questions.prompt: An Indiana licensed surveyor retires after 35 years of practice. Under 865 IAC 1-12-13, the surveyor's project records must be:

choices.text: Destroyed immediately to protect client confidentiality.
Retained for at least five years after the license is surrendered, and arrangements made for transfer to another licensed surveyor or the county surveyor.
Transferred only to a title insurance company.
Released to clients on request only; otherwise destroyed at the time of retirement.

questions.rationale: 865 IAC 1-12-13 imposes ongoing retention and transfer-on-retirement obligations. Records must be retained at least 5 years past surrender, and arrangements made to transfer them to another licensed surveyor or the county surveyor.

questions.prompt: In Indiana, when a surveyor finds, sets, or perpetuates a PLSS section corner, that surveyor must file a tie card with the:
questions.rationale: IC 36-2-12 directs the county surveyor to maintain the perpetual section corner record. Tie cards are filed there.

questions.prompt: Indiana subdivision plats generally require, in order:

choices.text: Final plat recording, then primary plan commission approval.
Primary plat approval, construction or bonding of improvements, secondary plat approval, then recording.
Only county surveyor approval, no plan commission step.
Recording first; plan commission review is optional in Indiana.

questions.rationale: Standard Indiana subdivision flow under the plat acts and local subdivision control ordinances: primary approval, improvements (or bond), secondary approval, then recording.

questions.prompt: On a boundary retracement, the surveyor's record research should at minimum include:

choices.text: The subject deed and adjoiner deeds, plus relevant plats, easements, prior surveys, and section corner tie cards.
Only documents listed on the title commitment.
The county assessor's parcel viewer only.

questions.rationale: A complete file includes the subject and adjoiners, plats, easements, prior surveys, and tie cards. Title commitments help but do not substitute for direct research.

questions.prompt: A title commitment is BEST characterized for the boundary surveyor as:

choices.text: A complete substitute for courthouse research.
A useful starting list that must be verified and supplemented by the surveyor's own research.
Authoritative only when it includes a survey exception.
Required only on ALTA surveys, never on Indiana boundary surveys.

questions.rationale: Title commitments aid the survey but are not authoritative for boundary determination. The surveyor must verify every cited document and identify any omitted record.

questions.prompt: An Indiana boundary plat is delivered to a client but bears no surveyor's seal. Under 865 IAC, the plat is:

choices.text: Valid for recording until the seal is added later.
Not a complete plat; the seal, signature, and license number are required for a recordable plat under 865 IAC.
Valid only if the client is also a licensed surveyor.
Valid because Indiana abolished the seal requirement in 1985.

questions.rationale: 865 IAC 1-12-11 requires the seal, signature, and license number on the plat. Without them the plat is not complete and cannot be recorded.

questions.prompt: A fence has occupied a line 4 ft inside the deeded boundary between two parcels for 35 years; both neighbors have always treated the fence as the boundary. The surveyor's correct treatment on a retracement is to:

choices.text: Move the boundary to the fence and certify the new line, since possession trumps deed.

Locate the deed line per the writings and show both the deed line and the long-standing fence, noting the apparent conflict in the surveyor's report; ownership of the disputed strip is a question for the courts, not the surveyor.
Refuse to complete the survey until the neighbors litigate.
Ignore the fence; only courts may consider occupation.

questions.rationale: The surveyor locates and reports; courts adjudicate ownership. Indiana doctrines (acquiescence, adverse possession) may have moved title, but the surveyor's role is to show the conflict, not resolve it.
